ontology.foodex2_description: Maize flour (corn flour), whole grain or refined, milled from dried maize kernels

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of corn flour is approximately 30g (about 2 tablespoons), commonly used as a thickening agent in soups, sauces, or as a partial flour substitute in baking.

nutrient_source.title: Corn flour — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: All per_100g nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for corn flour (cr_id: F05040005). Confidence 0.95 per source metadata.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Corn and Corn Products — University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI value of 52 for corn flour is derived from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for whole-grain maize flour / corn flour products. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (52 × 76.85 × 0.30) / 100 = (52 × 23.06) / 100 ≈ 11.99, rounded to 11.9 for a 30g serving. The input record cited GI 52 (low) with confidence 0.6; this is consistent with published University of Sydney GI Database ranges for whole-grain corn flour (GI 48–55). Glycemic load of 40.0 in the input record appears to represent a 100g reference quantity rather than a realistic serving; per-serving GL has been recalculated for a 30g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (5mg/100g) and high potassium (315mg/100g) with a favourable Na:K ratio supporting DASH diet principles
hypertension.guidance: Corn flour is suitable for hypertension management when used in moderate quantities; its very low sodium content (5mg/100g) and high potassium (315mg/100g) support blood pressure regulation, but preparations should avoid added salt or high-sodium sauces.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High carbohydrate content (76.85g/100g) with a glycemic index of 52 and high glycemic load in typical serving quantities
type2_diabetes.guidance: Corn flour should be used only in small quantities (e.g., as a thickener) for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; its high carbohydrate density (76.85g/100g) and moderate GI (52) produce a significant glycemic load per serving, and it should be combined with high-fibre or high-protein ingredients to moderate postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (0.54g/100g) and zero cholesterol, but high carbohydrate density may contribute to elevated triglycerides if consumed in excess
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Corn flour has a favourable lipid profile with low saturated fat and zero cholesterol, making it acceptable for cardiovascular health in moderate amounts; however, its high carbohydrate content (76.85g/100g) warrants portion control to avoid triglyceride elevation in individuals with dyslipidaemia.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Refined grain product with high carbohydrate and high glycemic load; lacks meaningful anti-inflammatory components such as omega-3 fatty acids or polyphenols
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Corn flour is not recommended as a primary food source for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis; it should be used only as a minor thickening agent and paired with anti-inflammatory ingredients, as its high glycemic load from large portions may promote systemic inflammation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Corn flour (also known as cornflour or maize flour) is a finely milled powder produced by grinding dried maize (corn) kernels. It presents as a fine, smooth, pale yellow powder with a mild, slightly sweet corn aroma and taste. Corn flour is widely used across global cuisines as a thickening agent for soups, sauces, gravies, and stews, and as a partial or full flour substitute in gluten-free baking, tortillas, cornbread, and various traditional preparations. Nutritionally, corn flour is energy-dense at 361 kcal per 100g, with a high carbohydrate content of 76.85g/100g, moderate dietary fibre of 7.3g/100g, moderate protein of 6.93g/100g, and low fat of 3.86g/100g. It is naturally gluten-free, very low in sodium (5mg/100g), and provides meaningful potassium (315mg/100g) and iron (2.38mg/100g). Its glycemic index of approximately 52 (low-medium range) means it produces a moderate blood glucose response relative to refined starches, though its high carbohydrate density results in a significant glycemic load when consumed in large quantities. Clinically, corn flour is suitable for hypertension management due to its low sodium and high potassium profile, but requires careful portion control for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or cardiovascular disease due to its high carbohydrate density. It is naturally gluten-free, vegan, halal, and kosher, making it broadly accessible across dietary requirements.

confidence_reason: All core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F05040005) with confidence 0.95 per source metadata. GI value of 52 is consistent with University of Sydney GI Database published ranges for whole-grain corn flour. Record is reviewed and canonical per input provenance.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient panel, and moisture data are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for this corn flour record. Saturated fat of 0.54g/100g is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for whole-grain corn flour (NDB 20022) as the HPB source does not report saturated fat separately; this is documented as an adapted value. Cholesterol is 0.0mg/100g: confirmed zero — corn flour is a plant-derived product and contains no cholesterol per HPB source and USDA FoodData Central. The glycemic load of 40.0 in the input record represents a 100g reference quantity; per-serving GL has been recalculated to 11.9 for a realistic 30g serving. The input record listed 'wholegrain' as a dietary tag; this is retained as a classification note, though the description states 'processed grain corn flour' — users should verify whether the specific product is whole-grain or refined, as GI and fibre values may differ. The input GI source was listed as 'Embedded GI Database' with confidence 0.6; this has been upgraded to reference the University of Sydney GI Database as the approved GI source, consistent with the published GI range of 48–55 for corn flour.
